title: Chapter 30: Fifty More
content: Two weeks after the no, and the world hasn't ended.

This is surprising. Not because Chloe expected the world to end—she's not that dramatic, even in capybara form—but because she genuinely didn't have a plan for what comes after you turn down financial salvation with your face. She'd been so focused on the decision that she'd forgotten to prepare for the aftermath. The aftermath being: same apartment, same frozen revenue, same Debra Wu, same five-week clock that's now a three-week clock. But also: same community. Same content. Same self.
She takes the wins where she can.

The #CapybaraMethod has metastasized—in the good way, if metastasis can be good, and honestly, in the wellness space, the metaphor tracks. Twelve cities now. Twelve cities where people are sitting in parks and community centers and one very optimistic person's studio apartment in Denver, doing the thing Chloe accidentally invented: existing on purpose, together.

Jordan organized eight of those twelve. The other four sprouted on their own, which is either proof that the method is filling a genuine need or proof that the internet can turn anything into a movement, and probably both.

Jordan is here today. In Dave's apartment, standing in the kitchen with a laptop bag over one shoulder and dark circles under their eyes that are visible from across the room, which is saying something because Jordan has dark skin and those circles have to work overtime to show up this clearly.

"Twelve cities," they say, and their voice has the cracked quality of someone who's been talking a lot and sleeping a little. "We hit twelve. Seattle's launching next week. Miami reached out—Miami, Chloe. People in Miami are doing the method. In the sun. Capybaras would love Miami."
"I've considered relocation," Chloe says. It gets a smile. Small, but real.

Jordan's smile is different these days. Tighter. Like the muscles remember the shape but haven't had time to practice. They're twenty-two. Chloe keeps forgetting that. Not because Jordan seems older—they don't, they seem exactly twenty-two, which is to say simultaneously ancient and infantile—but because Jordan operates at a tempo that makes Chloe feel like she's watching someone burn through their twenties in real time.

"Jordan has a thing they want to discuss," Dave says. He's making tea, which is his way of signaling that a real conversation is about to happen. Dave only makes tea when things matter. The rest of the time it's coffee or nothing.

"I have a thing," Jordan confirms. They pull a folder from their laptop bag. An actual paper folder. With tabs. And printed sheets. And a spreadsheet that, even from across the counter, Chloe can see has color-coded columns.
"What is this?" Chloe asks.

"A proposal." Jordan opens the folder, spreads the pages on the counter. "For formalizing #CapybaraMethod. Nonprofit status. Local chapters with official organizers. An annual conference—small at first, maybe two hundred people, but scalable. I've mapped out a five-year growth trajectory. Revenue model is donation-based with sliding-scale event fees. We could apply for grants. There's arts funding, wellness funding, community-building funding—I've identified twelve potential sources."
Their hands are shaking.

Not a lot. Not dramatically. Just a fine, persistent tremor that makes the papers flutter slightly when they point to the color-coded columns. Jordan notices Chloe noticing and presses their palms flat against the counter, stabilizing.

"I've been working on this for ten days," they say. "Since the Serene offer fell through. Because we need structure. We need something that can survive without any one person. If you—when you figure out the legal stuff, when the revenue comes back, we should have an organization ready to receive it. Something legitimate. Something that can't be claimed by a Debra Wu or bought by a Serene Wellness. Something that belongs to everyone."

Dave is listening very carefully. He's stopped making tea. The kettle is whistling—he doesn't turn it off. Chloe reaches over and clicks the stove off with her teeth, which is a move she's been perfecting for weeks and still feels vaguely heroic every time.
"Jordan," Dave says. "This is impressive."
"Thank you."
"How many hours did you sleep last night?"
"That's not—"
"How many?"
Jordan's jaw tightens. "Three. But I napped—"
"In the last week. Total."
"Dave—"
"What's your average this month?"

Jordan doesn't answer. Their hands are still pressed flat against the counter, and now the trembling is more visible because they're trying so hard to stop it. Chloe can see the tendons standing out, the effort of holding still.
Dave moves to the kitchen table and pulls out a chair. "Sit down."
"I don't want to sit down."
"Sit down, Jordan."

They sit. But it's not graceful—it's the kind of sitting that happens when your legs make the decision before your brain does. Jordan drops into the chair and their eyes close for half a second too long, and in that half-second Chloe sees the exhaustion naked on their face: the hollows, the pallor, the slight unfocus of someone running on stimulants and terror and the absolute refusal to stop moving.
"When's the last time you just sat down?" Dave asks. Not accusatory. Gentle. The gentlest Chloe has ever heard him.

"I can't." Jordan's eyes open. "If I sit down—I start *thinking*. The whole point is no thoughts, right?" A laugh that isn't a laugh. "I just keep going. If I keep going, I don't have to feel it. I don't have to sit with it. I don't have to—"
They stop. The tremor moves from their hands to their shoulders.
"That's not what it means," Dave says.
The kitchen is very quiet.

"It's not about not feeling things," Dave continues. "The method. Whatever Chloe accidentally invented. It's not about numbing out. It's about feeling things and not running from them. It's sitting with the discomfort of being a body in a world that doesn't want you to rest. It's—God, I don't know. I'm a sound engineer. But I know it's not *this*." He gestures at the folder, the spreadsheets, the shaking hands. "This is the opposite. This is all thoughts, no fluff."
Jordan looks at him. Their face does something complicated—defense and recognition fighting for the same features.

"I just wanted to build something," they say. "Before the corporations get it. Before Serene Wellness puts up a billboard that says #CapybaraMethod and sells gummies. I wanted there to be something real first. Something that can't be bought."
"I know," Dave says. "But you can't build a refuge by burning yourself down for the bricks."
Jordan swallows hard. Nods. Doesn't cry, but only in the way that twenty-two-year-olds don't cry, which is to say they absolutely do cry, just later, alone, in a way they'll pretend didn't happen.
They pick up the folder. "I'll leave these with you. When you're ready—if you're ready—we can talk about next steps. No rush."
"Jordan—"

"No, you're right." They stand up. The trembling has migrated to their voice now, a slight waver on the consonants. "You're right. I'll sleep. I'll take a day. I'll—" They're already moving toward the door. "Thank you. For the tea. Even though there was no tea."
"I'll make it next time," Dave says.
Jordan leaves.

The elevator in Dave's building is slow. Ancient. The kind of elevator that shudders and groans and makes you consider the structural integrity of cables you can't see. Jordan stands inside it, alone, doors closing, and pulls out their phone.

Force of habit. The thing they do every time they're alone: scroll. Check. Monitor. See what the method is doing, what people are posting, whether the community is still there, whether it's still theirs.
The #CapybaraMethod feed loads.
The first three posts are normal—meetup photos, breathwork tips, someone's capybara meme with the caption "me existing on purpose." The fourth post is an ad.
**SERENE WELLNESS 🌿 Discover the #CapybaraMethod with our new Ashwagandha + L-Theanine Calm Gummies. Authentic calm. Authentically you.**

The ad has Chloe's TikTok thumbnail. Not Chloe's actual image—they don't have rights to that, not yet—but a stock photo of a capybara, mouth open, mid-chew, framed in Serene Wellness brand colors. Below it: a link. A discount code. A funnel.
Jordan's face crumbles.

Not all at once. It happens in stages—the way a cliff face goes, or a relationship, or a person. First the forehead tightens. Then the mouth stops trying to hold its shape. Then the eyes fill, and spill, and keep spilling, because Jordan is in an elevator with nowhere to go and no one to perform for, and the relief of that, the sheer relief of not having to be the organizer, the grown-up, the one with the spreadsheet— It cracks them open.

They're twenty-two. They haven't slept more than four hours a night in weeks. They built something real because they were terrified of losing it, and here is the proof that you can build something real and it can still be stolen—not the thing itself, but the words, the hashtag, the aesthetic. The shape of it. The parts that fit on a billboard.
The elevator reaches the ground floor. The doors open. Jordan stands there for a long moment, phone in hand, screen still showing the ad.
Then they wipe their face with their sleeve. Straighten their shoulders. Step out into the lobby.
The lobby is quiet. Outside, the city is not. The city is never quiet. The city is the opposite of the method—relentless motion, no permission to stop, everyone performing the exact amount of okay.
Jordan walks through the lobby. Pushes through the door. Steps into the noise.
They do not look at their phone again. But they do not throw it away, either. They carry it, like everyone carries the thing that hurts them: close, habitual, impossible to set down.

Dr. Patel comes by that evening. She says it's a friend visit. She brings wine—good wine, the kind that comes in a bottle with a dog on the label, which she insists is thematically appropriate—and she sits on Dave's couch and drinks a glass and doesn't take any notes.
"Off the books," she says, pointing at Dave. "Friend. Not a vet. Not even a little bit."
"Noted."
"Chloe, come here."

Chloe comes. She's used to this by now—the hands that check her pulse, feel her joints, press gently along her spine. Dr. Patel's touch is warm and professional and slightly anxious, which is the correct way to touch a giant rodent who used to be a human and whose medical status is, charitably, unprecedented.

"Blood work from last time looks good," Dr. Patel says. "I ran it through the lab as a—I think I called it a 'wildlife conservation screening.' Which is technically not a lie." She pauses. "Whatever you are, Chloe, you're healthy. That's all I can say officially."
"And unofficially?"
Dr. Patel glances at Dave. He's in the kitchen, giving them space, pretending to clean something that's already clean.

"Unofficially, I have no idea what's happening to you. Your bloodwork reads like a healthy capybara. Your reflexes read like a healthy capybara. But your cognition, your communication, your sense of self—none of that is capybara. You shouldn't be able to do what you do. Type. Understand contract law. Say no to a hundred-thousand-dollar deal with your *nose*." She shakes her head. "I can't explain it. I just know that whatever you are, your body is sustaining it. For now."
For now. Chloe hears the qualifier. She thinks she's supposed to find it ominous, but actually she finds it honest. *For now* is the most honest thing anyone can say about anything.
"Thank you," Chloe says. "For coming. For the wine. For the not-being-a-vet."

"Anytime." Dr. Patel squeezes her shoulder—her actual shoulder, the capybara one, which is the size of a throw pillow and covered in coarse brown fur. "And Chloe? That email you sent. The 'no thank you.' Dave told me."
Chloe waits.
"That was the healthiest thing you've done since I've known you. Don't let anyone tell you it wasn't worth it."

She leaves. Dave finishes cleaning the already-clean counter. The apartment settles into its evening quiet—the hum, the traffic, the particular stillness of a space that has held too many feelings in too few weeks.
Chloe goes to her cushion. Lies down. Closes her eyes.
Somewhere in the city, twelve groups of strangers are sitting down together. Breathing. Existing. Doing the thing she didn't mean to invent and can't control and definitely, definitely can't protect.
Somewhere else, Serene Wellness is running ads against her hashtag.
Somewhere else, Debra Wu is probably drafting her next filing.
Somewhere else, Jordan is not sleeping.
No thoughts, just fluff.
The capybara tries.
